codeReview.summary: I reviewed the feature metadata, worker handoff, transcript skeleton, the compiler-worker skill, the mission/shared-state docs, and commit `ec0c4cb81eb6e3b16084a7653a09a7dd8a879145`. The runtime fixture itself covers the contracted `GET /items`, `POST /items`, unsupported-method, and port-in-use flows, but the newly added compiler support for module-level `let`/`let mut` is still order-dependent in typechecking. Resolver/global collection treats top-level lets as file-scope globals, yet typecheck only makes an `ItemLet` visible after it has already walked earlier `ItemFn` items. That means a handler or helper declared before the shared-state binding will fail typechecking even though the binding is meant to be globally reachable, so the feature's compiler slice is incomplete.

issues.description: `typecheck_exn` folds `check_item` over `prog.items` in source order, but `collect_type_info` explicitly skips `ItemLet` entries (`lib/typecheck.ml:2047`). As a result, `check_fn_decl` at `lib/typecheck.ml:2184-2186` only sees module-level bindings that appeared earlier in the file. Resolver already collects `ItemLet` into the global environment (`lib/resolver.ml:392-396`), so the new module-level state support is inconsistent across phases: a natural program layout like `fn handle_items(...) { items.push(...) }` followed later by `let mut items: Vec<String> = Vec::new();` resolves but then fails typechecking because `items` is missing from the type environment. That makes the advertised module-level/global shared-state support incomplete for this feature.

summary: Fail. The HTTP CRUD fixture and runtime behavior look correct, but the supporting compiler change is incomplete: module-level `let` bindings are only visible to functions that appear later in the file, so shared global CRUD state is still order-dependent and can break reasonable programs within the intended slice.
